subject: Physics
q: An inductor of self inductance 1 H connected in series with a resistor of $100 \pi \mathrm{ohm}$ and an ac supply of $100 \pi$ volt, 50 Hz. Maximum current flowing in the circuit is ________ A.
question: An inductor of self inductance 1 H connected in series with a resistor of $100 \pi \mathrm{ohm}$ and an ac supply of $100 \pi$ volt, 50 Hz. Maximum current flowing in the circuit is ________ A.
solution:
Impedance of circuit
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^2}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\omega_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(100 \pi)^2+(2 \pi \times 50 \times 1)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(100 \pi)^2+(100 \pi)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{2} \times 100 \pi \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{V}{2}=\frac{100 \pi}{\sqrt{2} \times 100 \pi}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\max }=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=1 \text { Ampere }
\end{aligned}$
Correct Answer : 1

subject: Physics
q: A telegraph line of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> has a capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and it carries an alternating current at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> kilo cycle per second. If minimum impedance is required, then the value of the inductance that needs to be introduced in series is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>. (If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>10</mn></msqrt></math>)
question: A telegraph line of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> has a capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and it carries an alternating current at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> kilo cycle per second. If minimum impedance is required, then the value of the inductance that needs to be introduced in series is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mH</mi></math>. (If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>10</mn></msqrt></math>)
solution:
The frequency given is 0.5 kilo cycle per second ⇒f=500 Hz⇒ω=2πf=1000π rad s-1.

Total capacitance of the wire is C=0.01×10-6×100 F.

Impedance is minimum at resonance. Therefore, for minimum impedance

XL=XC

⇒ωL=1ωC⇒L=1ω2C=110002×π2×0.01×10-6×100=10-1 H=100 mH

subject: Physics
q: In an $L C R$ circuit shown in the following figure, what will be the readings of the voltmeter across the resistor and ammeter if an a.c. source of $220 \mathrm{~V}$ and $100 \mathrm{~Hz}$ is connected to it as shown?
question: In an $L C R$ circuit shown in the following figure, what will be the readings of the voltmeter across the resistor and ammeter if an a.c. source of $220 \mathrm{~V}$ and $100 \mathrm{~Hz}$ is connected to it as shown?
solution:
In case of series RLC circuit, Equation of voltage is given by
$$
\mathrm{V}^2=V_R^2+\left(V_L-V_C^2\right)
$$
Here, $V=220 V ; V_L=V_C=300 \mathrm{~V}$
$$
\therefore V_R=\sqrt{V^2}=220 \mathrm{~V}
$$
Current $i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220}{100}=2.2 \mathrm{~A}$

subject: Physics
q:
A series LCR circuit driven by 300 V at a frequency of 50 Hz contains a resistance R=3 kΩ, an inductor of inductive reactance XL=250π Ω and an unknown capacitor. The value of capacitance to maximise the average power should be:
take π2=10
question:
A series LCR circuit driven by 300 V at a frequency of 50 Hz contains a resistance R=3 kΩ, an inductor of inductive reactance XL=250π Ω and an unknown capacitor. The value of capacitance to maximise the average power should be:
take π2=10
solution:
Angular frequency, ω=2πf=2π×50=100π rad s-1

At resonance,

XC=XL⇒1ωC=ωL⇒1ωC=250π⇒C=1100π×250π F=4 μF

q: A power transmission line feeds input power at<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>2300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> to a step-down transformer with its primary windings having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4000</mn><mo> </mo></math>turns giving the output power at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. If the current in the primary coil of the transformer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and its efficiency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>%</mi></math>, the output current would be:
question: A power transmission line feeds input power at<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>2300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> to a step-down transformer with its primary windings having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4000</mn><mo> </mo></math>turns giving the output power at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. If the current in the primary coil of the transformer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and its efficiency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>%</mi></math>, the output current would be:
solution:
Efficiency, η=0.9=PsPp

VsIs=0.9 × VpIp

Is=0.9 ×2300 ×5230=45 A

subject: Physics
q: When resonance is produced in a series LCR circuit, then which of the following is not correct?
question: When resonance is produced in a series LCR circuit, then which of the following is not correct?
options: Current in the circuit is in phase with the applied voltage.
Inductive and capacitive reactances are equal.
If $\mathrm{R}$ is reduced, the voltage across capacitor will increase.
Impedance of the circuit is maximum.
solution:
Impedance $(Z)$ of the series LCR circuit is
$Z=\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}$
At resonance, $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}$
Therefore, $Z_{\text {minimum }}=R$

subject: Physics
q:
An ac current is represented as
$\mathrm{i}=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \mathrm{Amp}$
The r.m.s value of the current is
question:
An ac current is represented as
$\mathrm{i}=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right) \mathrm{Amp}$
The r.m.s value of the current is
solution:
$i=5 \sqrt{2}+10 \cos \left(650 \pi t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\mathrm{i}^2=50+100 \cos ^2\left(650 \pi \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$+(2)(5 \sqrt{2})(10) \cos \left(650 \pi t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\left\langle\mathrm{i}^2\right\rangle=50+\frac{100}{2}+0$
$\left. \lt \mathrm{i}^2\right\rangle=100$
$ \lt \mathrm{i} \gt =10 \mathrm{Amp}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: In an LCR series circuit, current is maximum at resonance.
Statement II: Current in a purely resistive circuit can never be less than that in a series LCR circuit when connected to same voltage source.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: In an LCR series circuit, current is maximum at resonance.
Statement II: Current in a purely resistive circuit can never be less than that in a series LCR circuit when connected to same voltage source.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
Statement-I $I_m=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}}$ at resonance $X_L=X_C$ Thus, $I_m=\frac{V_m}{R}$ $\because$ Impendence is minimum therefore $I$ is maximum at resonance.
Statement-II $\mathrm{I}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)$ in purely resistive circuit.

question: The AC voltage across a resistance can be measured using a:
solution: The AC voltage can be measured by hot wire voltmeter. It uses the heating effect of the current.

subject: Physics
q: In an a.c. circuit, voltage and current are given by: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> respectively. The average power dissipated in one cycle is :
question: In an a.c. circuit, voltage and current are given by: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> respectively. The average power dissipated in one cycle is :
solution:
Standard expression of voltage is given by, V=V0sinωt and for current is given by, I=I0sinωt+ϕ. Clearly, ϕ is the phase difference here. From the given expression, ϕ=π3.

Average power dissipated in one cycle is given by,

 Pavg=VrmsIrmscos(ϕ)

=1002×100×10-32×cosπ3

=1042×10-3×12

=104=2.5 W

subject: Physics
q:
As per the given graph, choose the correct representation for curve A and curve B
{Where XC= Reactance of pure capacitive circuit connected with A.C. source
XL= Reactance of pure inductive circuit connected with A.C. source
R= Impedance of pure resistive circuit connected with A.C. source
Z= Impedance of the LCR series circuit }
question:
As per the given graph, choose the correct representation for curve A and curve B
{Where XC= Reactance of pure capacitive circuit connected with A.C. source
XL= Reactance of pure inductive circuit connected with A.C. source
R= Impedance of pure resistive circuit connected with A.C. source
Z= Impedance of the LCR series circuit }
solution:
The formula to calculate the inductive reactance is

XL=ωL   ...1

The formula to calculate capacitive reactance is

XC=1ωC   ...2

As can be seen from equation (1), the inductive reactance varies directly with the frequency and equation (2) indicates that capacitive reactance varies inversely with the frequency.

Hence, curve A represents the capacitive reactance and curve B represents the inductive reactance.

subject: Physics
q: In series LCR circuit, the capacitance is changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>C</mi></math>. To keep the resonance frequency unchanged, the new inductance should be :
question: In series LCR circuit, the capacitance is changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>C</mi></math>. To keep the resonance frequency unchanged, the new inductance should be :
solution:
As resonance frequency remains unchanged, therefore ω'=ω

⇒1L'C'=1LC

⇒ L'C'=LC

⇒L'4C=LC

⇒L'=L4

∵ Inductance must be decreased by 3L4.

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit, the magnitude of VL and VC are twice that of VR. Given that f=50 Hz, the inductance of the coil is 1 Kπ mH. The value of K is _____ .
question: In the given circuit, the magnitude of VL and VC are twice that of VR. Given that f=50 Hz, the inductance of the coil is 1 Kπ mH. The value of K is _____ .
solution:
Since the voltage across the inductor and capacitor is same, the circuit is in resonance. Therefore, the current in the circuit will be,

i=VR=2205=44 A.

The voltage across the inductor is double that across the resistor. Therefore, VL=2VR=440 V.

We know that VL=iXL=i×2πfL⇒L=44044×100π=110π H=100πmH

Therefore, K=1100=0.01≈0.

subject: Physics
q: For a given series LCR circuit it is found that maximum current is drawn when value of variable capacitance is $25 \mathrm{nF}$. If resistance of $200 \Omega$ and $100 \mathrm{mH}$ inductor is being used in the given circuit. The frequency of ac source is ______ $\times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$. (given $\pi^2=10$ )
question: For a given series LCR circuit it is found that maximum current is drawn when value of variable capacitance is $25 \mathrm{nF}$. If resistance of $200 \Omega$ and $100 \mathrm{mH}$ inductor is being used in the given circuit. The frequency of ac source is ______ $\times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$. (given $\pi^2=10$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{L} \times \mathrm{C}}} \\ & \mathrm{f}_0=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{100 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 10^{-9}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{25 \times 10^{-10}}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 5} \times 10^5 \times \sqrt{10} \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & =\frac{100}{10} \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & \mathrm{f}_0=10 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: An emf of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> to a circuit containing in series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> inductor and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mtext>Ω</mtext></math> resistor. The ratio of the currents at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>∞</mi></math> and at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mtext>s</mtext></math> is close to: (Take <math><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>7.389</mn></math> )
question: An emf of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> to a circuit containing in series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> inductor and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mtext>Ω</mtext></math> resistor. The ratio of the currents at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>∞</mi></math> and at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mtext>s</mtext></math> is close to: (Take <math><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>7.389</mn></math> )
solution:
i=i01-e-tL/R

=2051-e-t0.01/5
=41-e-500t

now at t=∞
i∞=4

at t=40 s
i40=41-e-500×40
=41-1e210000=41-17.2910000
i∞i40≈1 Slightly greater then one.

subject: Physics
q: A series LCR circuit is connected to an ac source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The circuit contain a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and an inductor of inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>79</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The capacitance of the capacitor needed to maximize the average rate at which energy is supplied will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μF</mi></math>.
question: A series LCR circuit is connected to an ac source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The circuit contain a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and an inductor of inductive reactance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>79</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The capacitance of the capacitor needed to maximize the average rate at which energy is supplied will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μF</mi></math>.
solution:
The average rate at which energy supplied i.e. power delivered is maximum in case of the resonance, therefore

XC=XL

⇒1ωC=79.6 Ω

⇒   C=1ω×79.6=12πf×79.6=1100π×79.6=40  μF

Hence, C=40 μF.

subject: Physics
q: Consider the LR circuit shown in the figure. If the switch S is closed at t=0 then the amount of charge that passes through the battery between t=0 and t=LR is:
question: Consider the LR circuit shown in the figure. If the switch S is closed at t=0 then the amount of charge that passes through the battery between t=0 and t=LR is:
solution:
The current in the LR circuit at any time t is
I=ER1-e-RtL

Where E, R, L, I are the EMF, resistance, inductance and the current respectively.
∴ Charge flown through the circuit from time 0 to LR is 
(charge) ∆q=∫0LRI.dt=∫0LRER1-e-RtLdt
⇒∆q=ERt+LR e-RtL0LR
⇒∆q=ERLR+LRe-1-LR
⇒∆q=ER×LeR
⇒∆q=ELeR2=EL2.7R2

subject: Physics
q: A circuit connected to an ac source of emf <math><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> in seconds, gives a phase difference of <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> between the emf <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> and current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math> . Which of the following circuits will exhibit this?
question: A circuit connected to an ac source of emf <math><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> in seconds, gives a phase difference of <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> between the emf <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> and current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math> . Which of the following circuits will exhibit this?
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> circuit with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> circuit with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> circuit with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> circuit with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>
solution:
ω=100 rad/sec 

phase difference between e and i is given by 

tanθ=XR           ∴θ=π4

so   tanθ=tanπ4=1

in case of RL circuit  X=XL=ωL

And in case of RC circuit  X=Xc=1ωc

For option A: R=1000 Ω and

             XC=1ωC=11001×10-6=10000 Ω
              tan⁡θ=XCR≠1
For option B: R=103 Ω and

                        XL=Lω=10-3100=0.1 Ω

                       tan⁡θ=XLR≠1
For option  C:R=1000 Ω and

                   XL=ωL=10010=1000 Ω
                    tan⁡θ=XLR≠1
For optionD:R=1 kΩ=1000 Ω and

                XC=1ωC=110010×10-6=1000 Ω
               tan⁡θ=XCR=1;θ=45o

subject: Physics
q: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is connected to a resistor. The time taken by the current to change from its maximum to the rms value is :
question: An <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is connected to a resistor. The time taken by the current to change from its maximum to the rms value is :
solution:
i=i0cos(ωt)
i=i0 at t=0
i=i02 at ωt=π4
t=π4ω=π4(2πf)=18f
t=1400=2.5 ms

subject: Physics
q: The figure given below shows an $LCR$ series circuit with two switches $S_1$ and $S_2$. When switch $S_1$ is closed keeping $S_2$ open, the phase difference $(\phi)$ between the current and source voltage is $30°$ and phase difference is $60°$ when $S_2$ is closed keeping $S_1$ open. The value of $(3L_1 - L_2)$ is _______ H.
question: The figure given below shows an $LCR$ series circuit with two switches $S_1$ and $S_2$. When switch $S_1$ is closed keeping $S_2$ open, the phase difference $(\phi)$ between the current and source voltage is $30°$ and phase difference is $60°$ when $S_2$ is closed keeping $S_1$ open. The value of $(3L_1 - L_2)$ is _______ H.
solution:
From the source $v = V_0 \sin(300t)$, the angular frequency is $\omega = 300\text{ rad/s}$.
Capacitance $C = 100\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-4}\text{ F}$, so $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$.

Case 1 ($S_1$ closed, $S_2$ open):
Closing $S_1$ short-circuits $L_2$, leaving an LCR series circuit with $C$, $R$ and $L_1$. With phase difference $\phi_1 = 30^\circ$:
$\tan 30^\circ = \dfrac{\omega L_1 - X_C}{R}$
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\omega L_1 - X_C}{R} \Rightarrow R = \sqrt{3}\,(\omega L_1 - X_C)$ ... (1)

Case 2 ($S_2$ closed, $S_1$ open):
Closing $S_2$ short-circuits $L_1$, leaving an LCR series circuit with $C$, $R$ and $L_2$. With phase difference $\phi_2 = 60^\circ$:
$\tan 60^\circ = \dfrac{\omega L_2 - X_C}{R}$
$\sqrt{3} = \dfrac{\omega L_2 - X_C}{R} \Rightarrow \omega L_2 - X_C = R\sqrt{3}$ ... (2)

Substituting $R$ from (1) into (2):
$\omega L_2 - X_C = \sqrt{3}\,(\omega L_1 - X_C) \cdot \sqrt{3}$
$\omega L_2 - X_C = 3\omega L_1 - 3X_C$
$2X_C = 3\omega L_1 - \omega L_2 = \omega(3L_1 - L_2)$

Therefore:
$3L_1 - L_2 = \dfrac{2X_C}{\omega} = \dfrac{2}{\omega^2 C}$

Substituting $\omega = 300\text{ rad/s}$ and $C = 10^{-4}\text{ F}$:
$\omega^2 C = (300)^2 \cdot 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^4 \cdot 10^{-4} = 9$

$3L_1 - L_2 = \dfrac{2}{9}\text{ H}$

Hence, the correct option is $(2)\ \dfrac{2}{9}$.

q: The alternating current is given by, i=42sin2πTt+10 A. The R.M.S. value of this current is __________ A.
question: The alternating current is given by, i=42sin2πTt+10 A. The R.M.S. value of this current is __________ A.
solution:
The given function of current is, i=42sin2πTt+10 A

It means,  i2=42sin22πtT+100+2042sin2πtT

⇒i2=422+100

RMS value of the current is, irms=i2=121=11 A

The amplitude of the oscillating displacement current for the applied <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mtext>Take </mtext><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>

C=∈0 A d=ϵ0×12×10-3 F

∴ XC=2×10-325×9×109=1825×106 Ω
∴ i0=V0XC=20×2518×10-6 A=27.47 μA.

The value of amplitude of displacement current will be same as value of amplitude of conventional current.
Hence option 3.

q: Two identical capacitors A and B, charged to the same potential 5V are connected in two different circuits as shown below at time t=0. If the charge on capacitors A and B at time t=CR is QA and QB respectively, then (Here e is the base of natural logarithm)
question: Two identical capacitors A and B, charged to the same potential 5V are connected in two different circuits as shown below at time t=0. If the charge on capacitors A and B at time t=CR is QA and QB respectively, then (Here e is the base of natural logarithm)
solution:
Charge on capacitor after charging with a battery of potential V, Q=CV

Now, capacitor A is connected as shown in the figure.

As diode is in reverse bias, so capacitor charge remains same after time t=CR.

∴ QA=CV

In case B, capacitor is connected as shown in the figure.

Here, diode is in forward bias, so capacitor discharges and its charge decays as 

QB=Q·e-tRC

where, t=RC

q: In the given figure, the charge stored in 6μF capacitor, when points A and B are joined by a connecting wire is _______μC.
question: In the given figure, the charge stored in 6μF capacitor, when points A and B are joined by a connecting wire is _______μC.
solution:
At steady state, capacitor behaves as an open circuit and current flows in circuit as shown in the diagram.

Req=9 Ω

i=9 V9Ω=1 A

ΔV6Ω=1×6=6 V

VA=3 V

So, potential difference across 6μF is 6 V.

Hence, Q=CΔV

=6×6×10-6 C

=36 μC
